prompt: Standard Precautions in dentistry mean staff should:

options.text: Treat all blood and body fluids as potentially infectious and use appropriate PPE/hand hygiene
Use PPE only when a patient discloses HIV
Wash gloves between patients instead of changing them
Skip hand hygiene if gloves were worn

explanation: Standard Precautions assume unknown infectious status; gloves do not replace hand hygiene.

distractorExplanations.d: Hand hygiene is required after glove removal.

prompt: When is an N95 respirator typically indicated in dental settings (concept)?

options.text: For every brief hallway conversation with healthy staff
For procedures/situations with airborne risk per CDC/facility policy (e.g., certain airborne diseases), after fit testing
Instead of eye protection during splash procedures always
Only as a fashion accessory

explanation: Respirators are for airborne hazards; splash protection still needs eyewear; fit testing matters.

distractorExplanations.a: Routine talk does not require N95.
distractorExplanations.c: Eye protection is separate for splashes.

prompt: Hand hygiene should be performed:

options.text: Only at the end of the workday
Only if hands look dirty
Before donning gloves, after removing gloves, and whenever hands are contaminated
Never when using alcohol rubs is banned by all policies always

explanation: Moments for hand hygiene surround glove use and patient care contacts.

distractorExplanations.d: ABHR is often allowed when hands are not soiled.

prompt: Which PPE combination is most appropriate for a procedure likely to produce splash of blood/saliva?

options.text: Gloves only
Shoe covers only
No PPE if the patient rinsed with mouthwash
Gloves, mask, and eye protection (goggles/face shield); gown when soiling of clothing is likely

explanation: Mucous membrane and clothing protection match splash risk.

distractorExplanations.b: Shoe covers do not protect face/hands.
distractorExplanations.c: Mouthwash does not eliminate blood/saliva risk.

prompt: Utility gloves in instrument processing are worn to:

options.text: Protect hands during cleaning of contaminated instruments and handling chemicals
Replace sterile surgical gloves during surgery
Be worn home for dishwashing
Touch charts and phones throughout the clinic day without removal

explanation: Heavy-duty utility gloves protect during decontamination; they are not sterile surgical gloves.

distractorExplanations.d: Cross-contamination to clean areas is improper.

prompt: Masks should be changed when:

options.text: Once a week regardless of use
They become wet, soiled, or between patients per protocol
Only if the patient requests a new color
Never, because moisture improves filtration

explanation: Wet masks lose barrier effectiveness; change based on use/contamination.

distractorExplanations.c: Color preference is not the infection-control rule.

prompt: Which statement about latex allergy risk reduction is correct?

options.text: Force latex gloves on known latex-allergic staff
Powdered latex is always the safest option
Use non-latex alternatives when indicated and minimize unnecessary latex powder exposures
Allergy history is irrelevant in dentistry

explanation: Identify allergies and choose appropriate glove materials.

prompt: After removing PPE, the next critical step is usually:

options.text: Touch your eyes immediately to check vision
Apply lotion containing contamination from gloves
Shake gloves vigorously to aerosolize debris
Perform hand hygiene

explanation: Hand hygiene after PPE removal reduces self-contamination.

prompt: Which item is considered a critical instrument (Spaulding) requiring sterilization?

options.text: Instruments that penetrate soft tissue or bone (e.g., surgical instruments, scalers that enter sterile areas)
Intact blood-pressure cuff used on unbroken skin only
Waiting-room magazines
Computer keyboards that never contact mucosa

explanation: Critical items contact sterile tissue/vasculature and require sterilization.

prompt: Semicritical instruments (contact mucous membranes) should be:

options.text: Only wiped with a dry cloth
Heat-sterilized when heat-tolerant; if heat-sensitive, use high-level disinfection per IFU
Rinsed with water only
Stored visibly soiled for the next patient

explanation: Semicritical items need sterilization or HLD depending on material tolerance.

prompt: When should gloves be changed during patient care in dentistry?

options.text: Only at the end of the workday
Never if using the same brand of gloves
Between patients and whenever torn/contaminated during a procedure per protocol
Only when the patient requests it

explanation: Gloves are single-use barriers; change between patients and when integrity is compromised.

distractorExplanations.d: Patient preference is not the standard.

prompt: Hand hygiene is required:

options.text: Only after lunch
Never if gloves will be worn
Only once each morning
Before donning gloves and after removing gloves, and whenever hands are visibly soiled

explanation: Hand hygiene is the core of standard precautions and is needed around glove use.

distractorExplanations.b: Gloves do not replace hand hygiene.

prompt: A surgical mask in dentistry is primarily intended to:

options.text: Protect mucous membranes of the wearer from splash/spatter and reduce droplet exposure
Sterilize the operatory air
Replace high-volume evacuation
Allow reuse all week if not wet

explanation: Masks are PPE for splash/droplet; they are not sterilizers and are not week-long single items.

distractorExplanations.d: Masks are typically changed when wet/soiled or between patients per protocol.

prompt: Eye protection should be worn:

options.text: Only during panoramic imaging
When splash or spatter of blood/saliva is reasonably anticipated
Never with patients under 18
Only if the dentist asks once a year

explanation: Standard precautions call for eye protection with anticipated splash/spatter.

distractorExplanations.d: Annual asks are not the rule.

prompt: If a glove tears mid-procedure, the correct approach is generally to:

options.text: Continue carefully with the torn glove
Tape the tear and finish the appointment
Remove gloves, perform hand hygiene, and don new gloves before continuing
Switch to bare hands for better tactile sense

explanation: Compromised gloves must be replaced after hand hygiene.

prompt: The correct general sequence for reusable instruments is:

options.text: Transport, clean, package, sterilize, store
Sterilize first, then clean heavily soiled instruments
Store dirty, then sterilize without cleaning
Package before removing bioburden

explanation: Cleaning must precede sterilization; soil interferes with sterilization.

distractorExplanations.c: Dirty storage then sterilize skips cleaning.
distractorExplanations.d: Packaging dirty items is wrong order.

prompt: Ultrasonic cleaners are used to:

options.text: Sterilize instruments to SAL 10^-6 alone
Remove debris via cavitation before sterilization
Disinfect operatory counters as the primary method
Sharpen burs automatically

explanation: Ultrasonics clean; they do not replace sterilization for critical instruments.

prompt: Instrument cassettes help primarily by:

options.text: Eliminating the need for cleaning
Replacing sterilization indicators
Keeping sets together and reducing sharps handling injuries during processing
Allowing mixed clean/dirty storage

explanation: Cassettes improve safety/organization but cleaning/sterilization still required.

prompt: Chemical indicators on packages show:

options.text: That bioburden is zero with certainty from color change alone
That the autoclave never needs biologic testing
Patient blood type
That sterilizing conditions (e.g., temperature/steam exposure parameters the indicator is designed for) were present—not necessarily that every item is sterile alone

explanation: External/internal chemical indicators are process monitors; biologic indicators verify sterilizer kill.

distractorExplanations.a: Color change ≠ absolute sterility proof alone.

prompt: Biological indicators (spore tests) should be used:

options.text: At least weekly (and with implantable device loads per CDC) to verify sterilizer lethality
Never, if chemical indicators are used
Only once when the sterilizer is purchased
Instead of cleaning instruments

explanation: CDC recommends routine BI monitoring; implant loads need BI results before use when required.

distractorExplanations.b: Chemical indicators do not replace BI.

prompt: A positive spore test (failed BI) should lead to:

options.text: Ignore it if packages look dry
Take the sterilizer out of service, recall/reprocess per policy, and do not use loads until failures are resolved
Continue using the sterilizer for surgical instruments only
Switch to cold sterile soak for all critical items indefinitely without investigation

explanation: Failed BI indicates possible sterilization failure—quarantine and investigate.

distractorExplanations.c: Surgical instruments are highest risk—do not continue.

prompt: Flash/immediate-use steam sterilization should be:

options.text: Used for every instrument to save inventory
Used for packaged implant trays stored for weeks
Used only for emergencies when no sterile replacement exists—not as a routine convenience
Preferred over cleaning

explanation: IUSS is for urgent situations with strict controls—not routine processing.

distractorExplanations.a: Inventory planning should avoid routine IUSS.
distractorExplanations.b: IUSS items are for immediate use.

prompt: When loading a sterilizer, packages should be:

options.text: Packed as tightly as possible to maximize count
Wrapped in aluminum foil as the primary wrap always
Placed wet into sterile storage immediately without drying when IFU requires dry packages
Arranged to allow steam/air circulation per IFU—not overloaded or stacked to block penetration

explanation: Steam must contact all surfaces; follow manufacturer loading instructions.

distractorExplanations.b: Foil is not a standard sterilization wrap system.

prompt: Hinged instruments should be processed:

options.text: Open/unlocked so sterilant contacts joint surfaces
Fully closed and clamped tightly always
Filled with cement before sterilization
Wrapped while visibly caked with debris

explanation: Open hinges allow steam/chemical contact at joints.

prompt: Which statement about event-related shelf life is correct?

options.text: Sterility lasts exactly 24 hours regardless of packaging
Sterile packages remain usable until an event compromises integrity (tear, moisture, expiration if dated), if storage is proper
Packages may be stored under sinks in puddles
Open packages can be resealed with tape for sterile use

explanation: Event-related sterility depends on package integrity and storage conditions.

prompt: The correct sequence for instrument reprocessing typically starts with:

options.text: Sterilizing first, then washing
Skipping cleaning if instruments look clean
Holding/cleaning (or cleaning) before sterilization
Oil sterilizing all plastics at 400°C

explanation: Soil must be removed before sterilization can be reliable.

distractorExplanations.b: Visual clean is not enough alone.

prompt: Ultrasonic cleaners are used to:

options.text: Sterilize instruments to SAL 10^-6 by themselves
Replace autoclaves for implant kits always
Disinfect operatory counters
Remove debris via cavitation before packaging/sterilization

explanation: Ultrasonics clean; they do not replace sterilizers.

prompt: Chemical indicators on packs primarily show:

options.text: That sterilizer process conditions for that indicator type were met — not that items are proven sterile by the indicator alone
That instruments are definitely sterile forever
Patient name and date of birth
That ultrasonic cleaning finished

explanation: Indicators monitor process conditions; biologic monitoring verifies sterilizer performance on a schedule.

distractorExplanations.b: Sterility assurance needs more than one chemical mark.

prompt: Hinged instruments should generally be:

options.text: Wired tightly closed for the cycle
Sterilized in the open/unlocked position so sterilant contacts all surfaces
Left dripping wet without drying when packaged
Stored closed in sealed pouches while wet

explanation: Open position improves sterilant contact on hinges.

prompt: A wet pack after a steam cycle should be:

options.text: Used immediately because moisture proves sterilization
Placed under a fan in the operatory for patients
Considered compromised for sterility assurance and reprocessed per protocol
Opened and air-dried then used without re-sterilizing

explanation: Wet packs can wick contaminants; reprocess.

distractorExplanations.a: Moisture is not proof of sterility.
distractorExplanations.b: OR fans are not the fix.
distractorExplanations.d: Opening then using without re-sterile is unsafe.

prompt: Clinical contact surfaces should be:

options.text: Barrier-protected and/or cleaned and disinfected between patients with an EPA-registered hospital disinfectant
Left visibly bloody for the next patient as a warning
Wiped with dry paper only always
Disinfected once a month if they look clean

explanation: Between-patient surface asepsis prevents cross-infection.

prompt: Housekeeping surfaces (floors, walls) typically require:

options.text: Sterilization with an autoclave daily
Routine cleaning; disinfection when contaminated with blood/body fluids per policy
Critical-item sterile technique for mopping
No cleaning ever in dental offices

explanation: Housekeeping surfaces are lower risk but still need cleaning and targeted disinfection when soiled.

prompt: Barriers (plastic covers) on light handles should be:

options.text: Used for a full week without change
Left in place when torn and wet with blood
Changed between patients and surfaces cleaned if contamination occurred
Considered a substitute for never cleaning under barriers

explanation: Barriers are single-patient; clean underlying surfaces on contamination/scheduled basis per protocol.

distractorExplanations.d: Cleaning under barriers is still needed periodically/when breached.

prompt: Dental unit waterlines should deliver water that:

options.text: May be heavily contaminated without concern because saliva is dirtier
Must be sterile water for all rinsing always without exception in every procedure type
Should never be flushed
Meets drinking-water standards for routine care (≤500 CFU/mL heterotrophic bacteria as a common benchmark) with monitoring/maintenance

explanation: DUWL maintenance and monitoring reduce biofilm risks; sterile water is required for certain surgical irrigations.

distractorExplanations.b: Surgical sterile water rules differ from routine rinsing.

prompt: Flushing waterlines at the start of the day and between patients helps:

options.text: Reduce planktonic bacteria in the lines
Sterilize handpieces alone
Replace the need for sterilizer BI tests
Eliminate the need for surface disinfection

explanation: Flushing lowers bacterial counts but is only one part of DUWL management.

prompt: Which waste is typically regulated as medical/biohazard waste in dentistry?

options.text: Office paper recycling mixed with needles
Sharps and items saturated with blood/saliva that drip when compressed, per local regulations
Uncontaminated paper towels from hand drying always as red-bag waste in all jurisdictions
Food wrappers from the break room

explanation: Regulated waste definitions are specific—sharps and saturated infectious items; follow local rules.

distractorExplanations.a: Needles do not go in paper recycling.
distractorExplanations.c: Clean paper towels are usually not red-bag.
distractorExplanations.d: Food waste is not clinical regulated waste.

prompt: Extracted teeth with amalgam should be:

options.text: Flushed into the city sewer routinely
Given to children as toys without disinfection discussion
Handled as potential biohazard and amalgam-containing waste per EPA/state rules—not incinerated as ordinary trash in ways that vaporize mercury improperly
Autoclaved in a manner that may vaporize mercury without proper controls

explanation: Amalgam/mercury disposal has special environmental rules; treated teeth may be returned under policies.

prompt: Spill cleanup of blood on a clinical surface should include:

options.text: Dry sweeping only
Alcohol wipe for one second always regardless of product IFU
No cleaning if the next patient is waiting
PPE, absorbent removal of bulk soil, then EPA-registered disinfectant with correct contact time

explanation: Clean then disinfect with proper wet time.

distractorExplanations.c: Turnover pressure does not justify skipping IPC.

prompt: Which statement about disinfection vs sterilization is correct?

options.text: Disinfection reduces pathogens on surfaces/items; sterilization destroys all microbial life including spores (within process capability)
They are identical terms
Disinfection always kills all spores on critical instruments
Sterilization is only for floors

explanation: Spaulding and CDC distinguish disinfection levels from sterilization.

distractorExplanations.c: Many disinfectants do not kill spores reliably.

prompt: Operatory cleanup between patients should prioritize:

options.text: Only emptying the trash once a week
Removing barriers, cleaning/disinfecting clinical contact surfaces, and managing sharps/waste safely
Mopping sterile fields into the autoclave
Spraying disinfectant into the air as fog for every room without surface wipe when IFU requires wipe methods

explanation: Systematic between-patient IPC protects the next patient.

distractorExplanations.d: Follow product IFU—many need surface wet contact.

prompt: Clinical contact surfaces that may be contaminated should be:

options.text: Wiped with plain water only once a week
Ignored if they look shiny
Barrier-protected and/or cleaned and disinfected between patients with an appropriate EPA-registered product
Covered with patient bibs reused all day

explanation: CDC/OSAP-aligned practice: barriers and/or disinfection between patients.

distractorExplanations.d: Reuse of contaminated covers is unsafe.

prompt: Housekeeping surfaces (floors, walls) typically require:

options.text: Sterilization after every patient
No cleaning in dental offices
Autoclaving mop heads between each operatory
Routine cleaning; more frequent disinfection when visibly soiled with blood/body fluids

explanation: Housekeeping surfaces are cleaned routinely; intensive disinfection when soiled.

distractorExplanations.c: Autoclaving mops each room is not standard.

prompt: Dental unit waterlines should be maintained so water quality:

options.text: Meets dental water standards (e.g., ≤500 CFU/mL heterotrophic bacteria for non-surgical use) via flushing/treatment protocols
Can be ignored if water tastes fine
Needs no monitoring once the unit is installed
Is only checked once per career

explanation: Waterline biofilms need active management and monitoring.

prompt: Regulated medical waste (e.g., sharps) must be:

options.text: Dropped loosely in regular trash if capped
Placed in appropriate puncture-resistant containers and handled per OSHA/state rules
Taken home by staff for disposal
Flushed down the operatory sink

explanation: Sharps and regulated waste have specific containment/disposal rules.

prompt: After a blood spill on a counter, you should:

options.text: Wipe once with a dry paper towel only
Spray perfume to mask odor
Contain, clean, and disinfect with an appropriate intermediate-level disinfectant per protocol
Ignore if smaller than a coin

explanation: Blood spills need proper cleaning/disinfection.

prompt: OSHA's Bloodborne Pathogens Standard requires employers to:

options.text: Establish an exposure control plan, offer HBV vaccination, provide PPE, and train employees
Ban all gloves to save money
Require employees to buy their own sharps containers from home
Eliminate handwashing sinks

explanation: BBP Standard mandates plans, engineering/work controls, PPE, vaccine offer, and training.

prompt: After a needlestick from a used instrument, the employee should:

options.text: Hide the injury to avoid paperwork
Wash the site, report immediately, and follow post-exposure evaluation/prophylaxis protocols
Apply concentrated acid into the wound
Finish the day of surgery first without reporting

explanation: Timely reporting enables PEP decisions and documentation.

prompt: Hepatitis B vaccination for at-risk dental employees is:

options.text: Optional for employers to ignore entirely without documentation pathways
Required to be paid fully by the employee always
Offered by the employer at no cost under OSHA BBP rules
Unnecessary because HBV has no occupational risk in dentistry

explanation: Employers must offer HBV vaccine; declination forms exist if refused.

distractorExplanations.a: Employers cannot simply ignore the offer requirement.
distractorExplanations.b: Vaccine is employer-paid when offered under OSHA.
distractorExplanations.d: HBV is a classic dental occupational risk.

prompt: Safety Data Sheets (SDS) provide:

options.text: Patient appointment histories
DEA schedules for controlled drugs only
Sterilizer BI spore counts automatically
Hazard information and safe handling/PPE guidance for chemicals

explanation: HazCom requires accessible SDS for hazardous chemicals used in the office.

prompt: Which is a work-practice control?

options.text: Recapping only with a one-handed scoop technique or safer method—not two-handed recapping
Installing a sharps container (engineering)
Writing the exposure control plan (admin document alone without practice)
Choosing wall paint color

explanation: Work-practice controls change how tasks are performed to reduce exposure.

distractorExplanations.c: Plans are administrative; practices enact them.

prompt: Eye wash stations should be:

options.text: Locked in a distant closet without signage
Available, accessible, and flushed/maintained per requirements when corrosive chemicals are used
Filled with soda as irrigant
Optional if bottled water exists somewhere in town

explanation: Prompt irrigation capability is required for chemical exposures.

prompt: Nitrous oxide occupational exposure is reduced by:

options.text: Running nitrous in unventilated closets for fun
Disabling scavengers to save gas
Scavenging systems, equipment checks, and appropriate ventilation/work practices
Holding the mask loosely near staff faces all day

explanation: Scavenging and maintenance protect staff from chronic N2O exposure.

prompt: Which training topic is required under OSHA BBP for dental employees with exposure risk?

options.text: Only marketing scripts for whitening upsells
Only how to brew coffee
Optional silent self-study with no documentation ever
Modes of transmission, PPE use, exposure response, and engineering/work-practice controls

explanation: BBP training covers exposure prevention and response; employers document training.

prompt: Regulated medical waste containers should be:

options.text: Labeled/color-coded, closable, and suitable to contain contents without leakage
Unlabeled clear bags mixed with lunch trash
Overfilled past the fill line routinely
Emptied by hand sorting needles

explanation: Proper containers prevent injury and illegal disposal.

prompt: Which statement about tuberculosis infection control in dentistry is correct?

options.text: TB is never a concern because dentistry uses gloves
Patients with infectious TB should have elective care deferred; urgent care uses airborne precautions/referral as appropriate
Coughing patients should wait in a crowded lobby indefinitely without masking policies
N95 use is never relevant

explanation: Airborne TB requires special handling beyond Standard Precautions alone.

distractorExplanations.a: Gloves do not stop airborne TB.

prompt: The OSHA Bloodborne Pathogens Standard requires employers to:

options.text: Ban all gloves to save cost
Prohibit reporting of needlesticks
Establish an exposure control plan and offer HBV vaccination to at-risk employees
Require employees to buy their own sharps containers

explanation: Exposure control plans and HBV offers are core OSHA BBP elements.

prompt: After a needlestick, the injured worker should generally:

options.text: Hide the incident to avoid paperwork
Squeeze the wound vigorously for 10 minutes then continue working
Wait a week to see if infection appears before reporting
Wash the site, report promptly, and follow post-exposure evaluation protocol

explanation: Prompt first aid, reporting, and evaluation matter for prophylaxis timing.

distractorExplanations.b: Aggressive squeezing is not recommended practice.

prompt: Engineering controls examples include:

options.text: Sharps containers and safer needle devices
Hoping staff remember to be careful only
Playing music loudly
Turning off operatory lights

explanation: Engineering controls remove hazards at the source.

distractorExplanations.b: Work practice alone is not engineering control.

prompt: Nitrous oxide occupational exposure is best reduced by:

options.text: Running nitrous without scavenger all day
Scavenging systems, good ventilation, and equipment checks for leaks
Holding the mask loosely away from the patient always
Ignoring odor because N2O is harmless at any level

explanation: Scavenging/ventilation reduce chronic exposure.

distractorExplanations.d: Chronic exposure is a recognized concern.

prompt: Employees with latex allergy risk should:

options.text: Double latex gloves for more protection
Powder latex gloves more heavily
Use appropriate non-latex alternatives and follow facility allergy protocols
Avoid reporting symptoms

explanation: Avoidance of latex and proper alternatives are key.
